county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Prowers County formed 1889 from Bent County. County seat is Lamar. Named for cattleman John Wesley Prowers. Santa Fe Railroad. Arkansas River valley. Sugar beet farming. Big Timbers Cheyenne/Arapaho heritage.
county_metadata.research_tips: Prowers County Clerk and Recorder in Lamar holds records from 1889 (created from Bent County). Lamar was a Santa Fe Railroad town on the Arkansas River. The Big Timbers Museum in Lamar has local history collections. The area was historically a gathering place for Cheyenne and Arapaho peoples at the Big Timbers cottonwood grove along the Arkansas. Sugar beet farming brought immigrant workers (German-Russian, Hispanic, Japanese). The Amache (Granada) Japanese American internment camp (1942-1945) was in neighboring Crowley County but many internee connections extend to Prowers County. Check Bent County for pre-1889 records. BLM GLO homestead records. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. The Lamar Daily News newspaper.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Prowers County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Pueblo County formed 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. County seat is Pueblo. Colorado's steel city (CF&I Bessemer steel works from 1881). Extraordinarily diverse immigrant communities. Hispanic heritage. 1921 Pueblo flood. Second largest city in southern Colorado.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pueblo County Clerk and Recorder holds records from 1861 as an original territorial county. Pueblo was Colorado's steel city -- the Colorado Fuel & Iron Company (CF&I) Bessemer steel works (from 1881) was one of the largest steel mills in the West. CF&I records at the Steelworks Center of the West and Bessemer Historical Society are critical for researching steelworker families (employment records, company housing, medical records). Pueblo's immigrant communities were extraordinarily diverse: Italian, Slavic (Slovenian, Croatian, Serbian), Hispanic, Greek, German, African American, and Japanese. The Rawlings Library's InfoZone in Pueblo has genealogical resources. El Pueblo History Museum documents the area's Hispanic and multicultural heritage. The 1921 Pueblo flood destroyed parts of downtown and some records -- check Colorado State Archives for surviving records. Catholic parish records (Our Lady of Mt. Carmel for Italians, Sacred Heart for Hispanics) are essential. Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Pueblo County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Rio Blanco County formed 1889 from Garfield County. County seat is Meeker. Named for the White River (Spanish: rio blanco). Meeker Incident (1879) led to Ute removal. Ranching. Oil and gas development.
county_metadata.research_tips: Rio Blanco County Clerk and Recorder in Meeker holds records from 1889 (created from Garfield County). Meeker was the site of the 1879 Meeker Incident (Ute uprising against Agent Nathan Meeker) which led to the Ute removal from western Colorado. The White River Museum in Meeker documents this history and has local collections. Ranching and later oil/gas development were the primary industries. Check Garfield County for pre-1889 records. The county has Ute history significant for Native American family research. BLM GLO records document homesteads and land patents. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. The Meeker Herald newspaper (from 1885) is a source for vital records and community events.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Rio Blanco County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Rio Grande County formed 1874 from Conejos and Costilla counties. County seat is Del Norte. Named for the Rio Grande headwaters. San Luis Valley gateway to San Juan mining region. Hispanic heritage. Denver & Rio Grande Railroad.
county_metadata.research_tips: Rio Grande County Clerk and Recorder in Del Norte holds records from 1874 (created from Conejos and Costilla counties). Del Norte was the gateway to the San Juan mining region and an early settlement in the San Luis Valley. Hispanic families trace to colonial New Mexican settlers. The Rio Grande County Museum in Del Norte has local collections. Monte Vista (a major San Luis Valley town) is in Rio Grande County. Potato farming attracted Japanese immigrant workers. The Denver & Rio Grande Railroad connected the valley to the rest of Colorado. Check Conejos and Costilla counties for pre-1874 records. Catholic parish records document Hispanic families. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. BLM GLO records for homesteads.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Rio Grande County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Routt County formed 1877 from Grand County. County seat is Steamboat Springs. Named for Territorial Governor John Routt. Ranching heritage. Coal mining (Oak Creek, Mount Harris). Ski resort (Steamboat). Norwegian skiing heritage.
county_metadata.research_tips: Routt County Clerk and Recorder in Steamboat Springs holds records from 1877 (created from Grand County). Steamboat Springs was a ranching town that became a ski resort. The Tread of Pioneers Museum in Steamboat Springs is an excellent local history resource. The county had coal mining at Oak Creek and Mount Harris (company towns). Ranching families like the Crawfords, Adairs, and Wheelers are well-documented. Carl Howelsen, Norwegian ski jumper, founded skiing in Steamboat Springs (1913). Check Grand County for pre-1877 records. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. BLM GLO records document homesteads and land patents. The Steamboat Pilot newspaper (from 1885) is a rich source for the community. Bud Werner Memorial Library has local history.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Routt County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Saguache County formed 1866 from Costilla and Lake counties. County seat is Saguache. Named from the Ute word for 'water at the blue earth.' San Luis Valley. Hispanic heritage. Baca Grant No. 4. One of the largest counties by area. Sparse population.
county_metadata.research_tips: Saguache County Clerk and Recorder holds records from 1866 (created from Costilla and Lake counties). One of the largest and least populated counties in Colorado. The San Luis Valley portion has Hispanic heritage predating Anglo settlement. The town of Saguache (originally Sagwatch) was a supply point for mining districts. Saguache County Museum has local collections. The Baca Grant No. 4 (a large Mexican-era land grant) is in the county. Crestone and the Baca Grande area have unique spiritual community history (modern era). Check Costilla and Lake counties for pre-1866 records. Catholic parish records serve the Hispanic community. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. BLM GLO records document homesteads and mining claims. The Saguache Crescent newspaper.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Saguache County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: San Juan County formed 1876 from Lake County. County seat is Silverton. Silver and gold mining. Durango & Silverton narrow gauge railroad. Most mountainous county in Colorado (no land below 9,000 feet). Diverse immigrant miners.
county_metadata.research_tips: San Juan County Clerk and Recorder in Silverton holds records from 1876 (created from Lake County). Silverton was a famous silver and gold mining town connected by the Denver & Rio Grande Railroad (the Durango-Silverton narrow gauge, now a tourist railroad). The San Juan County Historical Society Museum in Silverton has excellent mining-era collections. The county is the most mountainous in Colorado with no land below 9,000 feet. Diverse immigrant miners: Cornish, Irish, Italian, Finnish, Scandinavian, and Chinese. Silverton's Standard newspaper documents the mining era. The county had Ute history before Anglo settlement. Check Lake County for pre-1876 records. Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm. BLM GLO mining claims. Mining company records at Colorado State Archives and History Colorado.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for San Juan County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: San Miguel County formed 1883 from Ouray and San Juan counties. County seat is Telluride. Gold and silver mining. Western Federation of Miners labor history. Telluride labor wars (1901, 1903-1904). Diverse immigrant mining communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: San Miguel County Clerk and Recorder in Telluride holds records from 1883 (created from Ouray and San Juan counties). Telluride was a gold and silver mining town famous for its labor history -- the Western Federation of Miners organized here and the Telluride miners' strike of 1901 and the labor wars of 1903-1904 are well-documented. The Telluride Historical Museum has mining and labor history collections. Pandora, Tomboy, and Smuggler-Union mines were among the most productive. The county attracted Finnish, Cornish, Italian, and Austrian immigrant miners. Check Ouray and San Juan counties for pre-1883 records. Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm. BLM GLO mining claims. The Telluride Daily Journal newspaper documents the mining and labor eras.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for San Miguel County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Sedgwick County formed 1889 from Logan County. County seat is Julesburg. Named for Major General John Sedgwick. Smallest county in Colorado by area. Julesburg was an Overland Stage and Pony Express station. Burned by Cheyenne/Arapaho (1865). Union Pacific Railroad.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sedgwick County Clerk and Recorder in Julesburg holds records from 1889 (created from Logan County). Colorado's smallest county by area and one of the least populated. Julesburg was an historic overland stage and Pony Express station on the South Platte River -- the town was burned by Cheyenne and Arapaho warriors in 1865 and relocated multiple times. The Fort Sedgwick Historical Society and Depot Museum preserve this history. Union Pacific Railroad town. Check Logan County for pre-1889 records. BLM GLO homestead records are essential. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. Church records in Julesburg and Ovid document rural families. The Julesburg Grit-Advocate newspaper.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Sedgwick County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Summit County formed 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. County seat is Breckenridge. Gold mining (1859 rush). Originally enormous county; parent to Eagle, Garfield, Grand, Pitkin. Ski resorts (Breckenridge, Keystone, Copper Mountain, Arapahoe Basin).
county_metadata.research_tips: Summit County Clerk and Recorder in Breckenridge holds records from 1861 as an original territorial county. Breckenridge was a major gold mining town from the 1859 Pike's Peak gold rush through the early 1900s (placer and lode mining). Dillon, Frisco, and Montezuma were other mining camps. The original Summit County was enormous -- Eagle, Garfield, Grand, Pitkin, and parts of other counties were carved from it. The Summit Historical Society and Breckenridge Heritage Alliance have excellent mining-era collections. Dillon Reservoir (1963) flooded the original town of Dillon. The county transitioned to ski resort economy (Breckenridge, Keystone, Copper Mountain, Arapahoe Basin). Colorado State Archives has early territorial and county records on microfilm. BLM GLO mining claims and patents. The Summit County Journal newspaper (from 1880).
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Summit County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Summit County Deed Records -- County Clerk and Recorder
source_profiles.repository: Summit County Clerk and Recorder, Breckenridge
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books, mortgages, and liens for Summit County from 1861; County Clerk and Recorder maintains original records and indexes

source_profiles.source_name: Summit County Marriage Records -- County Clerk and Recorder
source_profiles.repository: Summit County Clerk and Recorder, Breckenridge
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and certificates for Summit County from 1861; Colorado required marriage licenses from territorial period; state vital records registration began 1907

source_profiles.source_name: Summit County Probate Records -- District Court / Colorado State Archives
source_profiles.repository: Summit County District Court / Colorado State Archives, Denver
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estates, guardianships, and probate proceedings for Summit County; District Court handles probate in Colorado; older records may be at Colorado State Archives on microfilm

source_profiles.source_name: Summit County Federal Land Patents -- BLM GLO Records
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Summit County including homestead entries and mining claim patents; searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov; mining claims are especially numerous in this county

source_profiles.source_name: Summit County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Summit County: 1870 (territorial), 1880-1950 (state); 1860 Kansas Territory census may include early settlers; 1885 Colorado state census is an important supplement between 1880 and 1890 federal censuses

source_profiles.source_name: Summit County Mining District Records and Claim Filings
source_profiles.repository: Colorado State Archives / Summit County Clerk and Recorder / BLM
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Mining district minutes, claim records, assessment work filings, and mining company records for Summit County; mining districts kept their own records before formal county recording; Colorado State Archives and History Colorado hold many mining-era records

source_profiles.source_name: Ute Nation Records -- Summit County Area
source_profiles.repository: NARA, Denver / Southern Ute Indian Tribe / Ute Mountain Ute Tribe / History Colorado
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Pre-removal Ute records for the Summit County area including treaty records, agency records, annuity rolls, and correspondence; Ute removal from western Colorado completed by 1881; Southern Ute and Ute Mountain Ute reservations in southwestern Colorado retain tribal records; NARA Denver holds federal Indian agency records
